title: SEGMENT 6 — Porcine endogenous retroviruses: the hitchhiker problem

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Porcine endogenous retroviruses: the hitchhiker problem
I want to take a detour now into a problem that is, in some ways, scarier than rejection.
Rejection kills the graft.
The problem I'm about to describe could, in principle, start an epidemic.

Pigs, like every other vertebrate on Earth, carry endogenous retroviruses — retroviruses that, sometime in the deep evolutionary past, infected an ancestor of the species, integrated their DNA into the host germ line, and have been passed down through every generation since.
Roughly eight percent of the human genome is endogenous retroviruses.
We carry them, you and I, in every cell.
Most are fragmentary and inert.
But some retain functional viral genes.
They're a permanent installation, written into the chromosomes, copied at every cell division.
Pigs carry their own set, called porcine endogenous retroviruses, or PERVs.
Every pig genome has them.
They sit at multiple locations on the chromosomes — anywhere from twenty-five to fifty copies, depending on the breed.
Most of them are dormant, but some retain the ability to produce viral particles.
In the right environment — pig cells in a dish, next to human cells in a dish — PERVs have been shown to jump species.
Pig cell makes a viral particle.
Particle drifts over.
Particle infects a human cell.
Human cell starts to express pig viral genes.
Sometimes the new infection integrates into the human cell's DNA.
You can see the nightmare scenario.

You take a pig organ — covered in pig endothelial cells, full of pig genome, and therefore full of PERVs — and you implant it inside a human being whose immune system is being deliberately suppressed by transplant drugs.
The pig cells inside the graft start producing PERV particles.
The particles infect the patient's own cells.
A new retrovirus is now resident in a human host.
The patient — and anyone the patient has contact with — could become the index case of a novel zoonotic retrovirus epidemic.
Like HIV, but starting from a hospital instead of a jungle.
Now, in fairness, the actual evidence for cross-species PERV transmission in living patients is thin.

Some early xeno experiments — including some perfusion procedures where patients' blood was passed through pig livers for therapeutic purposes — were followed up for years, and no PERV transmission was ever detected.
The risk may be lower than the worst-case scenarios.
But the possibility alone was enough to keep regulators awake at night.
For decades, the FDA's position was, essentially: until you can prove PERV won't jump, we won't approve a clinical xeno trial.
The breakthrough on this front came in 2017, and it's a story I want to spend a few minutes on, because it's also a CRISPR story.

The lab was George Church's at Harvard — yes, the same George Church we mentioned in the CRISPR lecture, who co-discovered mammalian CRISPR editing in 2013 — and the first author was a postdoc named Luhan Yang.
Yang would go on to co-found a company called eGenesis, which we'll come back to.
The Church-Yang team did something audacious.
They took pig fibroblasts.
They identified, in the pig genome, every functional copy of PERV — 25 copies in the cell line they were working with.

They designed CRISPR guide RNAs to a conserved sequence in the viral genome — the pol gene, which every functional PERV needs to be infectious — and they hit all 25 copies at once, simultaneously, with Cas9.
The result was a fibroblast cell line in which every PERV had been disabled.
Then they did the cloning step.
They took those PERV-free fibroblasts, transferred their nuclei into pig eggs, and made the first PERV-free piglets.
The 2017 paper in Science was titled, with refreshing directness, "Inactivation of porcine endogenous retrovirus in pigs using CRISPR-Cas9.
" The piglets were born.
They grew up.
PERV was, in their cells, undetectable.
The hitchhiker problem had a solution.
I want to pause and appreciate the technical achievement here.
Knocking out 25 copies of a gene at once — at 25 different chromosomal locations — was, in 2017, a record.
Most CRISPR experiments target one gene at a time.
The Church-Yang work showed that you could push the tooling to dozens of simultaneous edits in one cell, all integrated through one cloning step, all transmitted through the germ line.
That capability — multiplexed editing at scale — is what eventually made the 69-edit eGenesis pig possible.
But I also want to be honest about the limits of the PERV solution.
The Church 2017 work showed you could knock out PERV in fibroblast lines and produce viable piglets.
It did not yet show that those PERV-free piglets, when bred, kept the PERV out across generations — though follow-up work has shown that they do.
And it did not show that PERV-free organs are clinically safer than PERV-positive organs — we still don't have human data on that question.
What it did show is that the regulatory concern about PERV is, technically, addressable.
We can build a pig without functional PERV.
Now, an important footnote.
The pigs used in the 2022 Bennett heart transplant — the Revivicor 10-edit pigs — did not have the PERV knockout.
The FDA had concluded that with appropriate screening and monitoring of the donor herd, the residual risk of PERV transmission was acceptable.
The 2024 kidney patients, who received eGenesis pigs, did receive PERV-inactivated donors.
Both companies have taken different paths on this question, and we'll talk about the regulatory side of it in segment 14.
For now, what I want you to remember is this.
PERVs are the endogenous retroviruses every pig genome carries.
They could, in principle, jump into humans during transplant.
Church's 25-knockout pig eliminated them.
That's the headline.
It's a tour de force of multiplex CRISPR, it solved a fifty-year regulatory headache, and it's one of the foundations on which the modern clinical xenotransplant program is built.
Alright.
We've now covered the three big immune barriers — alpha-gal, the broader xenoantigen suite, and the PERV question — and the technological responses to each.
We have, in principle, a pig that can survive in a human body for at least a while.
Now I want to introduce the two companies actually building these pigs.
Because xenotransplantation, like a lot of modern biology, has moved from academic basic science into the corporate world, and the two companies are not interchangeable.
They have different histories, different pigs, and different bets.
